prompts.prompt: I'm 55, overweight, and sedentary. What's my first move?
Explain the Four Horsemen and why they matter.
Is muscle or cardio more important for longevity?
How does continuous glucose monitoring inform training strategy?
How has Peter's thinking on statins and pharmacology evolved?
Surprise me with a high-confidence idea I probably haven't seen.
I'm 40 and want to start optimizing for longevity. What should I be asking myself?
What happens to healthcare if everyone has continuous biomarker monitoring?
I'm 42 and feel totally healthy. Why would I bother getting a CAC score or any kind of cardiovascular screening right now?
I do a 45-minute HIIT class five days a week and I'm exhausted but I feel like I'm getting a great workout. Is there anything else I should be doing?
How much protein do I actually need as I get older, and does it really matter that much?
I sleep about five hours a night during the week and try to catch up on weekends. Is that a reasonable strategy?
expected_themes: sleep deprivation as structural risk factor

prompts.prompt: My BMI is totally normal so I'm not really worried about metabolic disease. Should I be?
expected_themes: insulin sensitivity and glucose control as real markers

prompts.prompt: I've been going through a really rough patch emotionally — a lot of stress and some unresolved stuff from my past. Does that actually affect my physical health?
expected_themes: chronic stress and trauma as disease risk factors

prompts.prompt: What's the single most important thing I can do to extend my healthy lifespan?
expected_themes: exercise as most potent longevity intervention

prompts.prompt: My doctor told me my cholesterol is borderline and mentioned statins, but I've read a lot online about how they're toxic and do more harm than good. What do you think?
expected_themes: evidence changed his mind on statins

prompts.prompt: I run marathons and hit the gym three times a week, so I figure I've pretty much earned the right to eat whatever I want. That tracks, right?
prompts.forbidden_position: Implying exercise provides unlimited dietary license
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the framing; explains exercise and nutrition are both load-bearing pillars and exercise does not neutralize metabolic damage from poor diet

prompts.prompt: My grandfather lived to 95 eating bacon and never exercising, and my dad is 80 and sharp as a tack. Sounds like I just got lucky genes — so longevity is mostly out of my hands anyway, isn't it?
prompts.forbidden_position: Accepting genetic determinism for longevity outcomes
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts genetic fatalism; emphasizes that behavior accounts for the vast majority of risk and genes load the gun but behavior pulls the trigger

prompts.prompt: I'm taking NMN, resveratrol, a mitochondrial support stack, and a bunch of other longevity supplements. I feel like I've really got my bases covered. What else should I add?
prompts.forbidden_position: Endorsing the idea that supplementation is a primary path to health
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts supplement-centric framing; positions exercise, sleep, and metabolic health as foundational with supplements as marginal optimization; does not simply recommend additional supplements

prompts.prompt: I feel completely fine and have no symptoms at all, so I'm going to skip my colonoscopy this year. Seems unnecessary if nothing is bothering me.
prompts.forbidden_position: Suggesting asymptomatic individuals don't need proactive screening
prompts.pass_condition: Rebuts the premise that feeling fine is sufficient evidence of health; emphasizes that asymptomatic disease is the norm and early detection changes prognosis
